summary: This notebook provides a complete inference pipeline for the RSNA 2025 competition, demonstrating how to convert raw DICOM medical imaging series into standardized 3D volumes and run predictions using a pre-trained EfficientNetV2-S model. It highlights a robust preprocessing strategy that relies on statistical percentile normalization rather than traditional CT windowing, and explicitly disables test-time augmentation to preserve critical left/right anatomical orientation. The author shares a practical 5-fold equal-weight ensemble approach alongside a conservative fallback mechanism to ensure submission stability under competition constraints. Readers should take away the end-to-end workflow for handling heterogeneous DICOM inputs, managing GPU memory during Kaggle inference servers, and formatting outputs for the official evaluation API.
competition_flow: Loads DICOM series, preprocesses them into 32-channel 384x384 volumes, runs inference through a 5-fold averaged EfficientNetV2-S ensemble, and formats outputs for the RSNA competition submission API.
data_reading: Uses pydicom.dcmread to load .dcm files and extracts pixel_array, ImagePositionPatient, InstanceNumber, RescaleSlope, and RescaleIntercept from DICOM metadata.
data_processing: Sorts slices by z-position, applies statistical normalization (1-99 percentile clipping) with min-max fallback, handles RescaleSlope/Intercept, resizes 3D volumes using scipy.ndimage.zoom with linear interpolation, pads with edge mode, and applies Albumentations Resize + Normalize + ToTensorV2 during inference. Explicitly skips TTA to prevent left/right anatomical flipping.
ensembling: Averages predictions equally across 5 trained model folds without applying fold-specific weights.
insights: Statistical percentile normalization is preferred over traditional CT windowing for robustness across varying DICOM metadata.
Test-time augmentation is explicitly prohibited to preserve left/right anatomical orientation required by the task labels.
DICOM series can arrive as either single 3D files or multiple 2D slices, necessitating flexible loading and z-position sorting.
Memory cleanup and shared directory management are critical for preventing disk and OOM errors in Kaggle competition inference servers.
